topic: sheep shearing day — 14 sheep this year

turns.content: shearing day. I am covered in lanolin up to my elbows and I love it
Ha, that sounds like a good day! How many are you shearing today? And are you doing it yourself or do you have help?

14 this year. my husband helps hold them and my nephew came out too so we have a little crew. we started at sunrise because you want to get it done before it warms up, you know. the sheep are calmer in the cold
Starting at sunrise is smart — calmer sheep and cooler working conditions for everyone. 14 is a solid morning's work with three people. Are you using hand shears or electric?

hand shears. the way my grandmother did it and her grandmother before that. electric is faster sure but I like taking my time with them. each one I know by name. you know old Susie she is the matriarch. she just stands there like a queen waiting for her spa treatment. doesn't even flinch

Susie sounds like she's got the routine down after years of this. There's something to be said for hand shears — you can feel the wool better and you're less likely to nick the skin. Do they all have names, all 14?

every single one. Susie is the oldest, then there's Trouble who earned her name the day she was born, she came out sideways. Dusty because she rolls in the dirt the second you turn around. Little Star has a white patch on her forehead. I could go on

I love that each name tells a story. Trouble coming out sideways — she was making her personality known from day one. How's the wool quality looking this year? The fleece can vary a lot depending on the season they've had.

real good actually. we had decent rain this summer so the graze was better than usual. the fleece is thick and clean. well, clean for sheep, you know. Dusty's fleece has got half the mesa in it as usual but the rest look great

Better graze really does show up in the fleece — healthier sheep, thicker wool. Sounds like Dusty is living up to her name right to the end. What are you planning to do with the wool? Sell it, or are you keeping some for weaving?

keeping most of it. some I'll card and spin myself. the rest I'll take to the trading post in Chinle. Susie's wool is always the softest so I keep hers separate. she gets the premium treatment because she gives premium fleece

That's a fair deal — Susie brings her best, she gets the best. Spinning your own yarn from sheep you raised and sheared yourself, that's the full cycle right there. How long does it take you to card and spin the wool from all 14?

oh it takes weeks. months really. I do it in the evenings after work when my hands need something to do. it's good for the mind you know. just sit there with the wool and the spindle and the world gets quiet. the rez has two seasons, mud and dust, and right now we're in mud season so I'll be inside spinning a lot

That sounds like real medicine — hands busy, mind quiet, working with something that connects you to your sheep and your grandmothers all at once. Mud season spinning sounds like the perfect winter routine. I hope the rest of the shearing goes smooth today.

ahéhee'. just Trouble and two more to go. Trouble is giving my nephew the runaround right now which is why I am on my phone instead of helping. he needs to learn, you know. she will teach him patience whether he likes it or not

Ha, Trouble doing what Trouble does best. Your nephew is getting the real education today — no classroom teaches patience like a sheep who doesn't want to sit still. Good luck with the last three. Enjoy the rest of the day with your flock.
